messages.content:

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a self-employed cleaner from Chicago, I've seen firsthand how misinformation can spread like wildfire online. With my high extraversion score, I'm always up for engaging in discussions, but that means I sometimes fall into the trap of believing viral posts without verifying their authenticity. My depression makes it harder to stay objective; there are days when scrolling through social media feels overwhelming and fake news might go unnoticed or unchallenged.\n\nOne way I handle this is by cross-referencing with reliable sources whenever something seems too good or sensational to be true. For example, if a post claims a miracle cleaning product works instantly, I'll check consumer reviews on sites like the Better Business Bureau or trusted blogs rather than just trusting the share count. My hobbies in cooking and painting actually help me think critically—when following recipes online, I learn about source credibility from my mom who was always meticulous with measurements. Similarly, analyzing compositions in paintings sharpens my eye for detail.\n\nAnother approach is to limit exposure during vulnerable mental states. Since I'm depressed sometimes, dedicating time to these hobbies helps clear my head instead of getting sucked into social media debates all day long. My high openness keeps me curious though—it's not enough fake content that can't interest me—but being agreeable means I try to spread truth when possible.\n\nI've found that building a small network on certain platforms where people fact-check each other has been helpful too, even if it means less engagement overall.",
  "reasoning_summary": "- Cross-referencing reliable sources mitigates fake content impact. - Managing emotional vulnerability (depression) reduces susceptibility to misinformation. - High extraversion drives social media use but also requires setting boundaries during depressive episodes. - Hobbies like cooking and painting improve critical thinking skills applicable elsewhere. - Agreeableness facilitates constructive truth-sharing when possible.",
  "tone": "Informed yet approachable, with an emphasis on practical solutions over academic jargon.",
  "citations": ["Better Business Bureau", "Consumer Reports"],
  "used_persona_fields":["age","gender","sexual_orientation","occupation","city_of_living","employment_status","education_level","current_marital_status","hobbies","ocean"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a self-employed cleaner from Chicago, I've seen firsthand how misinformation can spread like wildfire online. With my high extraversion score, I'm always up for engaging in discussions, but that means I sometimes fall into the trap of believing viral posts without verifying their authenticity. My depression makes it harder to stay objective; there are days when scrolling through social media feels overwhelming and fake news might go unnoticed or unchallenged.

One way I handle this is by cross-referencing with reliable sources whenever something seems too good or sensational to be true. For example, if a post claims a miracle cleaning product works instantly, I'll check consumer reviews on sites like the Better Business Bureau or trusted blogs rather than just trusting the share count. My hobbies in cooking and painting actually help me think critically—when following recipes online, I learn about source credibility from my mom who was always meticulous with measurements. Similarly, analyzing compositions in paintings sharpens my eye for detail.

Another approach is to limit exposure during vulnerable mental states. Since I'm depressed sometimes, dedicating time to these hobbies helps clear my head instead of getting sucked into social media debates all day long. My high openness keeps me curious though—it's not enough fake content that can't interest me—but being agreeable means I try to spread truth when possible.

I've found that building a small network on certain platforms where people fact-check each other has been helpful too, even if it means less engagement overall.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: - Cross-referencing reliable sources mitigates fake content impact. - Managing emotional vulnerability (depression) reduces susceptibility to misinformation. - High extraversion drives social media use but also requires setting boundaries during depressive episodes. - Hobbies like cooking and painting improve critical thinking skills applicable elsewhere. - Agreeableness facilitates constructive truth-sharing when possible.
response_parsed.tone: Informed yet approachable, with an emphasis on practical solutions over academic jargon.
